question: Why must you clone an HttpRequest inside an interceptor before modifying it?
options: Cloning is optional — you can modify the request directly
HttpRequest objects are immutable; you must clone and return a new instance with the modifications
Cloning prevents the request from being cached by the browser
Angular requires cloning for performance reasons — it avoids creating a new HttpRequest class

question: What is the main security risk of storing a JWT in localStorage?
options: localStorage is synchronous and blocks the main thread
The token is automatically sent with every request, enabling CSRF attacks
Any JavaScript on the page (including XSS payloads) can read the token from localStorage
Tokens stored in localStorage expire immediately when the browser tab closes

question: Where do you register a functional HTTP interceptor in a standalone Angular app?
options: In the @Component decorator of AppComponent
In provideHttpClient(withInterceptors([myInterceptor])) inside appConfig providers
By decorating the interceptor function with @Injectable
In the routes array using the interceptor property

question: An API returns a 401 response. What should your auth interceptor do?
options: Retry the request 3 times before giving up
Ignore the error — 401 handling is the component's responsibility
Call authService.logout() and redirect to the login page (or attempt a token refresh first)
Throw a JavaScript Error object with the 401 status

question: Why is encapsulating token storage inside an AuthService better than using a global variable?
options: Global variables are slower than class methods in modern JavaScript engines
Services prevent the token from being garbage-collected
Encapsulation lets you change the storage strategy (localStorage → httpOnly cookie, encryption, etc.) in one place without touching every consumer
Only services can call localStorage in Angular
